region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, South India and Sri Lanka; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour noodle disc with fresh grated coconut and palm sugar topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Putu Mayam / Idiyappam as of 2026. Closest parent would be a steamed rice flour product node; no exact match available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed rice flour string hopper (South Indian / Sri Lankan) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBS is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for steamed extruded rice flour noodle discs; no exact Putu Mayam / Idiyappam code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Putu Mayam or Idiyappam as of 2026.

preparation_method: served with fresh grated coconut and palm sugar

serving.description: 3 discs (~90g rice flour noodles + 20g fresh grated coconut + 10g palm sugar, ~120g total)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Putu Mayam is 3 steamed rice flour noodle discs (~120g total), comprising approximately 90g of rice flour noodles, 20g of fresh grated coconut, and 10g of palm sugar (jaggery), providing approximately 234 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Putu Mayam — HPB Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Putu Mayam. Values represent the composite dish (rice flour noodle discs with fresh grated coconut and palm sugar topping) per 100g as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — analogous estimate from steamed fine rice flour noodles (rice vermicelli / idiyappam structure)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Putu Mayam / Idiyappam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fine extruded steamed rice flour noodles (rice vermicelli), which have published GI values of 58–70 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The palm sugar topping (jaggery GI ~55) does not materially alter the overall GI at the quantities used. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 33.1g) / 100 ≈ 21.5. Available carbohydrate per 120g serving = 37.0 × 1.2 × (1 − dietary_fibre_adjustment) ≈ 33.1g net.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~20mg per serving); saturated fat from coconut (lauric acid) has neutral-to-slightly-beneficial cardiovascular effect
hypertension.guidance: Putu Mayam is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (~20mg per 120g serving); the saturated fat from fresh coconut is predominantly lauric acid (C12:0), which has a neutral-to-slightly-beneficial effect on blood pressure and cardiovascular risk compared to long-chain saturated fats.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-to-high GI (~65) and high glycemic load (~21.5 per serving); 18g total sugar per serving from palm sugar topping
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Putu Mayam with caution: the fine rice flour noodles have a GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of ~21.5 per standard serving (3 discs, 120g), which is classified as high GL; the palm sugar (jaggery) topping provides ~12g sugar per serving and does not offer a clinically meaningful glycaemic advantage over refined sugar at these quantities, making portion control and pairing with low-GI foods advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from fresh coconut (~4.2g per serving) is predominantly lauric acid; low sodium; no trans fat; low cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Putu Mayam presents a low cardiovascular concern: it contains no trans fat, negligible cholesterol, and very low sodium; the ~4.2g saturated fat per serving comes from fresh grated coconut (lauric acid, C12:0), which current evidence suggests has a neutral-to-slightly-beneficial effect on LDL/HDL ratio compared to long-chain saturated fats such as palmitic acid, though total saturated fat intake should still be monitored within daily limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Putu Mayam, also known as Idiyappam or Indian string hoppers, is a traditional Tamil Indian steamed rice flour dish widely consumed as a breakfast and morning snack in Singapore, Malaysia, South India, and Sri Lanka. The dish is made by pressing fine rice flour dough through a perforated mould (an idiyappam press) to form thin, spiralling noodle strands arranged into flat disc shapes, which are then steamed on woven rattan trays. Each serving consists of three discs (~90g rice flour noodles) topped with fresh grated coconut (~20g) and palm sugar or jaggery (~10g), totalling approximately 120g per serving. The texture is soft, delicate, and slightly chewy, with a mild rice flavour complemented by the sweetness of palm sugar and the richness of fresh coconut. Per 100g: 195 kcal, 2.5g protein, 4.0g fat (3.5g saturated, predominantly lauric acid from coconut), 37.0g carbohydrate, 15.0g sugar, 2.5g dietary fibre, 17mg sodium. Per standard serving (120g): approximately 234 kcal, 3g protein, 4.8g fat, 44.4g carbohydrate, 18g sugar, 3g dietary fibre, ~20mg sodium. Glycemic index ~65 (medium-to-high, analogous to fine steamed rice flour noodles); glycemic load ~21.5 per serving (high). The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and dairy-free. In Singapore, Putu Mayam is strongly associated with Tamil hawker stalls, Indian provision shops, and morning wet market vendors at Tekka Market, Dunlop Street hawker centres, and Serangoon Road. The Malay-influenced name 'Putu Mayam' is used in Singapore and Malaysia, while 'Idiyappam' is the Tamil and Sri Lankan name for the same preparation. Nutritional data sourced from HPB Singapore (Foodo). Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: Indian string hoppers calories per serving

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore (Foodo), the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest structurally equivalent food (fine steamed rice flour noodles / rice vermicelli), clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor with honest gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Putu Mayam dish (rice flour noodles + fresh grated coconut + palm sugar) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish contains no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no carotenoid-rich ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is vegan with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no dietary vitamin D sources present). GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on fine extruded steamed rice flour noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (published GI range 58–70 for rice vermicelli); no direct GI measurement for Putu Mayam / Idiyappam exists in the approved GI database. GL per serving = (65 × 33.1g available carbohydrate per 120g serving) / 100 ≈ 21.5. FoodOn URI is null: no exact FoodOn node exists for Putu Mayam / Idiyappam as of 2026. SNOMED CT code is null: no SNOMED CT code identified for this dish.
